attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with ongoing pain in her left ankle. She complains of many years of atraumatic, aching pain in the ankle that is exacerbated with movement. She has a medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. She has a 40 pack-year smoking history and drinks two glasses of wine at weekends. She was a high-level dancer in her 20s.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She has a BMI of 31 kg/m2. Her X-ray shows the presence of osteophytes on the lateral and medial malleoli, tibiotalar joint margins, severe joint space narrowing, and constant tibiotalar sclerosis.

She has trialled non-operative measures such as exercise, physiotherapy, walking aids and weight loss without success. She has also tried diclofenac, paracetamol, corticosteroid injections, and even opioids, but they have not helped sufficiently. The patient requests surgical management.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate surgical plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has late stage ankle osteoarthritis (OA), as indicated by her risk factors, symptoms, examination findings and radiological findings. Risk factors in this case include her age, her previous activities of dancing (with possible malleolar fractures), diabetes, and being overweight.

The grading system used to determine this is the Kellgren and Lawrence score modified for the ankle joint, which has been shown to be reliable when considered alongside clinical symptoms. The grading for the Kellgren and Lawrence score, based on radiological findings is:

  • Grade 1: Osteophytes of doubtful meaning on the medial or lateral malleolus, rare tibial sclerosis, joint space width unimpaired.

  • Grade 2: Definite osteophytes on the medial malleolus, joint space width unimpaired.

  • Grade 3: Definite osteophytes on the medial and/or lateral malleolus, moderate (<50%) joint space width narrowing

  • Grade 4: Definite osteophytes on medial and lateral malleoli as well as tibio-talar joint margins, severe (>50%) to complete joint space narrowing, constant tibio-talar sclerosis.

In this case the radiological findings describe grade 4 (late-stage) ankle osteoarthritis based on the presence of osteophytes on the lateral and medial malleoli, tibiotalar joint margins, joint space narrowing that is severe, and constant tibiotalar sclerosis.

The typical first line management for osteoarthritis is lifestyle modifications. However, the patient has tried these and they have not been successful. The next step would be to use pharmacological management, however this has also been insufficient. Third line management options are surgical, though it should be appreciated that these procedures carry some level of risk, particularly in a patient with medical comorbidity.

Arthrodesis is the gold-standard surgical management for end-stage ankle OA. This procedure involves fusing the bones of the ankle joint together, eliminating motion at the joint. The primary purposes of this procedure are to get rid of pain, to correct the deformity, and ensure a stable foot. It has very high rates of successful union, at approximately 85-100% (Abidi et al.), and is hence considered a highly reliable procedure. However, it is worth recognising that, with her smoking history, being overweight, and having diabetes, her success rate is likely to be much lower.

Arthroscopic joint debridement involves using small incisions and a camera to remove damaged cartilage, loose bodies, and inflamed synovial tissue from the joint. It is typically only used in limited circumstances for the surgical management of ankle OA, such as in early stages or in cases there is a clearly defined surgical target (such as anterior impingement due to anterior osteophytes or in the excision of loose bodies which are intra-articular).

Arthroplasty involves replacing the damaged joint surfaces with artificial components. However, for the ankle (unlike the hip and knee) the procedure tends to also require bony and soft tissue realignment procedures to restore a normal mechanical axis, and this can be difficult for older patients. As such arthroplasty is not currently the gold-standard surgical treatment for ankle OA.

Osteotomy involves cutting and realigning the bone to redistribute weight-bearing forces across the joint. It is not typically used in the treatment of osteoarthritis of the ankle. However, it may be used to restore joint alignment in cases where there is demonstrated fracture malunion, and may be hence be used in preventing the development of OA in some cases.

Bipolar shell allograft involves replacing the damaged cartilage surfaces with donor cartilage and bone. This may be used in some circumstances for end-stage ankle OA amongst individuals who are young, however is not recommended for individuals with ankle malalignment, and needs to be avoided in smokers and those who are cannot take immunosuppressing medications (such as our patient, who has diabetes).
learningPoints: The Kellgren and Lawrence score modified for the ankle joint is a grading system used, alongside consideration of clinical symptoms, to determine the severity of ankle osteoarthritis.
Unlike hip and knee osteoarthritis, the gold standard surgical management for ankle osteoarthritis is arthrodesis.
references: Adukia V, Mangwani J, Issac R, Hussain S, Parker L. Current concepts in the management of ankle arthritis. J Clin Orthop Trauma. 2020 May-Jun;11(3):388-398. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcot.2020.03.020

Holzer N, Salvo D, Marijnissen AC, Vincken KL, Ahmad AC, Serra E, et al. Radiographic evaluation of posttraumatic osteoarthritis of the ankle: the Kellgren-Lawrence scale is reliable and correlates with clinical symptoms. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2015 Mar;23(3):363-9. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25463444/
Abidi NA, Gruen GS, Conti SF. Ankle arthrodesis: indications and techniques. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2000 May-Jun;8(3):200-9. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10874227/

Herrera-Pérez M, Valderrabano V, Godoy-Santos AL, de César Netto C, González-Martín D, Tejero S. Ankle osteoarthritis: comprehensive review and treatment algorithm proposal. EFORT Open Rev. 2022 Jul 5;7(7):448-459. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1530/EOR-21-0117

Bloch B, Srinivasan S, Mangwani J. Current Concepts in the Management of Ankle Osteoarthritis: A Systematic Review. J Foot Ankle Surg. 2015 Sep-Oct;54(5):932-9. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26028603/
